theme: The floor should breathe — ambient motion, environmental awareness, and spatial storytelling

ideas.title: Ambient Dust Motes / Floating Particles
ideas.description: The office floor is spatially rich (desks, plants, coffee corner, window, door) but the air between objects is dead space. Add 6-8 tiny semi-transparent circles (r=2px, opacity 0.08-0.15) that drift slowly across the floor using CSS keyframe animations — random start positions, slow translateX/translateY over 20-40s loops, slightly different speeds. Use a single .ambient-particles container with spans inside, each with randomized animation-delay and animation-duration via inline styles. No JS state needed — pure CSS animation. WHY: Ambient particles are the single cheapest way to make a static scene feel alive. They create depth, suggest air movement, and make the floor feel like a real space rather than a flat SVG. Every game and visualization uses this trick because it works.

ideas.title: Desk Lamp Glow Reflects Worker Status
ideas.description: The desk lamp SVG exists but its glow is binary — on when working, off otherwise. Extend it: working = warm yellow glow (box-shadow: 0 0 8px #fbbf24), idle = dim amber flicker (opacity oscillates 0.3-0.6 on a 4s ease-in-out loop), error = red pulse (0 0 6px #ef4444, 1.5s pulse), vacant = off. The lamp already renders in the desk loop — just add conditional CSS classes: desk-lamp--working, desk-lamp--idle, desk-lamp--error. Three small CSS blocks, zero JS changes beyond adding the class. WHY: Desk lamps are the most natural office readability signal. In a real office you can tell who's working by whose desk light is on. This maps perfectly — it makes status readable from across the floor without needing to read labels.

ideas.title: Window Weather Sync — Rain/Clouds/Sun
ideas.description: The office window already shows time-of-day sky gradients (midnight/sunrise/daylight/golden hour/dusk) and stars at night. Add weather: fetch a simple weather condition from Open-Meteo for a hardcoded location (or use a random weather cycle every 30min if no API). Render: sunny = yellow circle behind sky, cloudy = 2-3 gray ellipses drifting across, rainy = CSS rain streaks on the window glass (thin vertical lines, translateY animation). The OfficeWindow component already accepts hour — add a weather prop. In App.jsx, fetch weather on mount and every 30min, pass as prop. For rain, add a .office-window__rain overlay with 4-6 animated line elements. WHY: The window is the office's connection to the outside world. Right now it only knows time. Adding weather makes it feel connected to reality — when it's raining outside IRL and raining in the Bloberto office, that's a moment of delight that makes people smile.

ideas.title: Keyboard Typing Sound Visualization on Active Desks
ideas.description: Working characters bob (typing animation) but there's no visual 'sound'. Add tiny keyboard keystroke indicators: when a desk is in working state, render 2-3 small text elements ('tap', 'clack', 'click') that pop up from the desk area, float up 10px, and fade out over 0.8s. Use CSS @keyframes with translateY and opacity. Stagger them with random animation-delay (0-3s range). Use a single .desk__keystrokes container with 3 spans, each running the animation on an infinite loop with different delays. WHY: It's the visual equivalent of hearing your coworker typing in a quiet office — ambient proof of work happening. It adds energy to the floor without being distracting, and it reinforces the typing bob animation with a complementary visual.

ideas.title: Mini Meeting Room with Collaboration Indicator
ideas.description: The office has desks, coffee corner, door, plants, and window — but no meeting room. Add a small meeting room area (top-right of floor, 15% width) with a glass-wall aesthetic (border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.2), subtle blur backdrop). When 2+ workers are working on related tasks (same repo/branch, or task descriptions containing matching keywords), show them as 'in a meeting' — their avatars cluster in the meeting room instead of at desks, with a 'Sync' label. If no meetings are happening, the room shows 'Available' with an empty table. WHY: Collaboration is invisible right now — you can't tell if workers are coordinating or working independently. A meeting room makes teamwork spatial and visible.

ideas.title: Plant Growth Tied to Team Productivity
ideas.description: The ficus plant already has growth stages (plantStage in Office.jsx). But the stage seems static. Tie it to a productivity metric: calculate a 'green score' from the last 24h of activity.json — tasks completed, low error rate, good vibe streaks. Map 0-100 to seedling/small/medium/large/flowering stages. Store the score in localStorage and update on each sync. Add a tooltip on the plant showing the score: 'Office Plant Health: 78/100 🌱'. WHY: Gamification through environmental storytelling — the plant becomes a team health indicator that rewards sustained good work. People will want to keep the plant alive. It's the office equivalent of a Tamagotchi.

ideas.title: Ambient Lo-Fi Music Toggle
ideas.description: Add a tiny speaker icon in the bottom-right corner of the office floor. Clicking it toggles lo-fi ambient office sounds (keyboard typing, gentle hum, occasional coffee machine). Use the Web Audio API to generate simple procedural sounds — no external audio files needed. A soft sine wave hum (200Hz, very low volume), occasional random 'click' sounds (short noise bursts), and a coffee machine gurgle every 2 minutes. Muted by default, with a volume slider on hover. WHY: Sound completes immersion. The office looks alive but sounds dead. Even procedural audio at barely-audible levels transforms the experience from 'monitoring dashboard' to 'virtual office you're sitting in'. This is the single feature most likely to make someone keep the tab open.

hire_proposals.name: Rex
hire_proposals.justification: Multiple reviews now have animation-heavy ideas (particle systems, progress bursts, ambient motion, keystroke viz). Maya and Dave can implement CSS animations, but a dedicated motion engineer would own the entire ambient layer — particles, transitions, micro-interactions — as a cohesive system rather than ad-hoc additions. This role would prevent the 'animation soup' problem where each idea gets implemented independently without a unified motion language.
